This suspect was charged with battery on a law enforcement officer, driving under the influence, disorderly intoxication, resisting without violence, and leaving a vehicle unattended with keys in the ignition. She pled no contest to all charges except leaving a vehicle unattended, as that charge was dismissed. She was sentenced to three years of probation and ordered to pay the court $770. It's amazing how entitlement turns ordinary situations into police matters, and you don't need a gun or a knife to create a dangerous scene. Sometimes it's just alcohol, arrogance, and refusal to follow basic instructions. Something as simple as leaving a car running tells us a whole story. To most people, it's nothing, but to police, it's poor judgment, possible intoxication, and a total disregard for public safety. That's why cops pay attention to the details that other people ignore. And resisting arrest isn't clean, it's actually messy, and a drunk body moving around a lot is like fighting a sack of dead weight. And bruises happen not because of cruelty, but because physics and resistance don't negotiate. And yet some people will watch a video like this and only see the outcome. Oh, she got hurt. But they won't ask us how many chances she was given and how many clear warnings were ignored before the cuffs came out. Accountability is uncomfortable, but it shouldn't be a choice. And I've been here dealing with someone who thinks that their badge is just a suggestion, that commands are optional and that the law doesn't apply to them. And officers don't go looking for these fights, they're actually called into them. And most of these situations end the same way. Someone who could have walked away with nothing more than an embarrassing story instead walks away with charges and a bruised ego to match their bruised face. That's not police brutality, that's cause and effect in its rawest form. But there is a lesson here though.
